title: Goldman Just Lit the Fuse: $920B AI Capex Is Coming — and That's the 'Conservative' Number
subtitle: Twelve hours after Oracle crashed 10% on a $70B capex plan, Goldman Sachs told investors the real AI infrastructure bill is $920 billion. The timing is either genius or sadistic.
summary: Goldman Sachs dropped a bombshell warning that AI capex could hit $920 billion by 2027 — and called that estimate 'likely too conservative.' The note landed literally hours after Oracle imploded 10% in after-hours trading on its own $70B capex announcement.

image.caption: Goldman Sachs headquarters at 200 West Street, New York

bodyHtml:
You know the vibes are cooked when Goldman Sachs drops a research note that basically reads: "What you just saw happen to Oracle? That's the trailer."

Here's the timeline. Wednesday after the bell, Oracle reports a record quarter — revenue up 21%, cloud screaming, IaaS up 93%. And the stock craters 10% in after-hours. Why? Because Oracle also dropped a $70 billion capex plan plus a $40 billion debt and equity raise. The Street looked at those numbers and ran. Record earnings, record revenue, record cloud growth — none of it mattered. The capex bill was the only thing anyone saw.

Then Thursday afternoon, Goldman Sachs strolls into the chat.

The $920 Billion Grenade

Goldman's analysts published a note calling for AI infrastructure spending to hit $920 billion by 2027. Not a typo. Nine hundred and twenty billion dollars. And here's the punchline: they warned that number is "likely too conservative."

Let that sink in. The market just watched Oracle shed $30 billion in market cap overnight over a $70 billion spending plan. And Goldman is telling everyone the real AI infrastructure tab is thirteen times bigger — and that's the low-end estimate.

Twelve hours after Oracle gets punished for a $70B capex bill, Goldman tells the world the real number is $920B. The math ain't mathing.

The Timing Is Brutal — and Maybe Intentional

This note didn't land in a vacuum. It landed on Day 5 of the worst tech rout in years. The S&P 500 has bled -4.4% in five sessions. SMCI got completely erased — -28% in a single day, -37% across the stretch. Qualcomm torched -21% in the same window. The chip complex is in free fall. The Nasdaq can't catch a bid.

And into this panic, Goldman drops a note saying the AI capex supercycle is just getting started — and it's going to cost almost a trillion dollars.

The cynic's read on this is obvious. Goldman Sachs doesn't just research markets — it trades them. Higher rates, chaos in tech, a rotation into financials — this is Goldman's weather. The GS trading desk is having a banner week while semiconductor investors are updating their resumes. The note about $920 billion in coming AI spend isn't a warning. It's a flex.

The Oracle Lens

The Oracle crash is the perfect case study for why Goldman's number is terrifying. Oracle didn't miss. Oracle beat. Cloud revenue up 47%. Earnings above consensus. And the stock still got smoked because the capex number scared people more than the revenue number excited them.

Goldman's note makes it crystal clear: the risks to AI stocks are "rising" — their word, not ours. When the most connected bank on Wall Street is flagging risk in AI names while simultaneously calling for a $920 billion spending wave, you're not reading analysis. You're reading the pregame show for a massive sector rotation.

The real question isn't whether $920 billion gets spent. It's whether investors are still willing to pay for it. Right now, the market is screaming no.

— The Signal
